Author: Simonw; Caches Archive Org Ghostarchive
Title: Vibe engineering
Text:
I'm hoping that in addition to arguing about why this term is stupid (see this note at the end of the article) we can also have a conversation about the meat of the article, which is the section that points out that tried and tested techniques from professional software teams are incredibly effective at getting better results from 2025's new collection of coding agents.
Version control, research up front, planning a detailed spec, minting, automated tests, test-first development, CI, automation, preview deploys... there are a lot of sophisticated engineering techniques that pay off enormously when working with coding agent tools.
Yes, several times now I've seen someone write a blog post with the breakthrough insight that LLMs work better in a codebase that's well-organized, uses strong typing, has good automated tests, comes with product requirement and design documents that are up to date (!) and explain the why not just the how…in other words, exactly what we all already knew human software projects should have! These are not "sophisticated" techniques, they're just what you should be doing for anything nontrivial.
Are humans just better at rationalizing how they can make progress without bothering to do all that stuff? Are LLMs going to start requesting a more "agile" process to save tokens?
Good software engineering practices are still pretty rare in industry from what I've seen over my career. I'm OK calling the top end of this scale "sophisticated" - if your team has continuous deployment and CI and automatic linting and code formatting and up-to-date documentation and good commit message habits and decent code review processes in place I think your team is likely top 10th percentile of how software gets built.
AI will be a net win if it manages to jedi mind trick some teams into having more disciplined engineering.
Are humans just better at rationalizing how they can make progress?
My guess is what we you are describing is an invisible knowledge gap being made visible.
I tend to think of an LLM as a good fresh hire. Sure, it can code, but can it code the right thing? With all the practices listed, it sure can. With those missing it starts floundering.
This is no different to a new human hire. They are spending much more time floundering than they are letting on because they don't want to look bad. Without docs and a good code base, they're going to flounder more for longer, bur they won't tell anyone.
LLMs take that invisible issue and push it right into your face and make it your problem. This is probably a long-term good thing.
In my experience an individual's expertise, the value they bring to a project, will tend to be in the domain where they spend the bulk of their time. If your goal is to become a manager, everything you're talking about sounds great. You'll spend the bulk of your time managing, management reflexes will become second nature to you, and you'll start to see the process as the product. I really liked the use of the word "legibility" to describe this kind of mindset about where value is created.
The downsides are likely to be pretty much the same as the downsides of outsourcing aggressively or leaning hard into bureaucracy in a human engineering organization: both are anathema to creativity. If your goal is (like me) to be a hacker, which is to say an artisan of code, you hone your skill and your creative muscle by going out and doing your craft. I choose to do this (without AI) because I want to create software that's wildly new and exciting: the kinds of tools most people have grown too cynical to even dream of being possible to build.
What irks me about the AI movement is that it has often accepted without question all its technical priors. To use an example close to home for me, all the AI IDE authors decided to just fork VSCode. They evidently thought that it must be the pinnacle of IDE evolution, their reasoning being "I need something that already works that I can fork". I could point out any of a million things about the way VSCode is designed that range from nonsense to insanity, but none of that matters when the real idea for the future is that it no longer matters if the IDE is very good because you won't be the one suffering through using it.
In sum you've got all these people who think they're out their blazing a trail to the future, but really they're all just wearing the AI blinders of isolation while moving as a pack down the same small set of already-well-beaten paths.
Here's the thing: I also want "to create software that's wildly new and exciting".
I plan to use AI to help me do that, because AI expands the scope of tools I can use.
I used to stick to my lane: I'd build software with Python and JavaScript because those were the two languages I was fluent in, and ramping up on another language and ecosystem could take me months to get productive.
Now? If the best way to solve a new problem is with Go, I'll use Go. My existing skills transfer far easier then before.
If I want to design an entirely new language I'm no longer resistant to the amount of stuff I'd have to master first. LLMs know Lex and Yacc and Lemon and dozens of other things in the parser/generator space. They'll bootstrap me to productivity, then I can move beyond what they can help with myself.
I see AI tools as a great liberator for my own personal ability to learn new things and take on more ambitious projects.
It still sounds to me like you've defined your goal in terms of a process not a result. Your objective is to get out of the lane you've been stuck in using Python and JS, and to achieve that objective you'll use LLMs to dabble in other languages. You want to write a programming language maybe.
I respect that as a display of curiosity, but I think it is not unreasonable to expect that your first challenge will be mastering those new-to-you technologies: making the noob mistakes and living with the results of messes you've made. Once you have mastery over the tools, then you can use them to do something visionary and different.
Once you have mastery over the tools, then you can use them to do something visionary and different.
That's where we disagree. I don't think you need mastery of a tool in order to do something visionary or different with it. You need to understand enough of that tool to achieve the visionary/different result.
I see the key thing here being around problem domains and ways of applying the tool. I could learn the tiniest thing about ffmpeg
and still be the first person to apply it to resolving some weird issue with underwater cameras and marine biology.
I don't think you need mastery of a tool in order to do something visionary or different with it.
A cursory glance at the history of music will illustrate this phenomenon. Arguably it's even the opposite: mastery of a tool can make it difficult to do something visionary or different with it. However, after the naïve visionary shows the way, the people who follow and put in the time to become masters of the new area can far surpass the original accomplishment.
I would distinguish between these two situations:
At this point, the "one-off" part is important, based on the amount of time I have to give feedback to an LLM because it's turning the codebase into an unmaintainable mess.
AI should be fairly adept at letting anyone with no skill or low skill do medium skill work like making one-off ffmpeg integration according to domain-specific needs. Why do you need to be the one to prompt it? Not that you shouldn't if you're really interested in marine biology, but it's just a rather short lever to stand on. You're likely to be adding lines of code: creating new complexity. What we really need experienced engineers for is identifying and eliminating unnecessary complexity that has already crept into our critical infrastructure.
You'll spend the bulk of your time managing, management reflexes will become second nature to you, and you'll start to see the process as the product.
Yeah, at my previous job, it became clear to me that to upper management (from the company that bought us out), the process of the process is to process the process to ensure the process has processed the process. And that's how the company I worked for went from "favorite vendor" to "why are we even partnering with these jokers?" in about a year (the customer in question was the Oligarchist Cell Phone Company).
Counterproposal, similarly tongue-in-cheek: if you want to coin a new term for "highly responsible engineering with AI support", maybe use words whose complexity matches the complexity of the task.
I mean, if someone tells me "I vibe-engineered this PR", I would assume that they did not actually adhere to all the (commendable!) engineering practices you listed, but just used the term because it sounds less cheap than "vibe-coding". But if someone says "I created this PR, adhering to the 11 well-known assisted-engineering practices (and my own practices as well)" my impression is: this is someone who is sufficiently meticulous to spell out the entire term, so there's hope that they put the same meticulousness into their engineering.
Yeah, that's a fair point. I'm enjoying the inherent contradiction of "vibes" and "engineering" here, but it's quite likely my odd sense of humor isn't going to translate well to the professionals I'm trying to influence here!
I'm still of the opinion that what is described is largely just software engineering – it's just that we've got new tools to play with.
The new way of building systems through orchestrating the agents might indeed deserve a new term, but I'd suggest it be scoped to the agents, e.g. agentic software development, as it's quite different from the highly interactive original definition of vibe coding.
Yes, there’s literally nothing different other than the amount of typing involved.
If we're talking about having a serious name, then it shouldn't have the term "vibe" in it. It's really silly, but not in a good or funny way. It's a really, really bad term.
Yeah, as a real engineer, who builds actual things that include life safety considerations, I'm going to ask you not to promote that terminology.
Have you seen Hillel Wayne's piece about that? https://www.hillelwayne.com/post/are-we-really-engineers/
Of course. I don't usually make such a distinction. I did today though. Why?
Not sure, you tell me.
I picked "vibe engineering" deliberately knowing that people sometimes like to gatekeep the term engineer, as a cheeky acknowledgement of the fact that I want to gatekeep here myself!
I want to emphasize that vibe coders who haven't written a line of code before in their life are doing a very different thing from experienced software engineers who use LLMs to enhance their existing professional practice.
Since you concede you are just being cheeky, why not grant my request?
What do you want me to do, delete my article?
The idea is out there now. I doubt I could stop it if I wanted to!
EOF
Slop is slop.
"Slop" is a useful term in that it helps differentiate AI-generated junk from stuff that was created with the help of AI that's genuinely useful and not a waste of time.
I see that word as serving an equivalent purpose to "spam". Spam is unwanted marketing content sent to people without their consent - it's not a global term for all forms of marketing content.
Same for slop - not all AI-assisted content is slop, but low quality, unreviewed and requested AI content absolutely is.
I wrote about this last year as the there was starting to gain momentum.
To me it's all spam/slop at this point. AI and AI content is shoved in my face everywhere I turn. I've had to use Microsoft 365 recently and it's shocking. I just wanted to read an email but I'm redirected to some sort of Copilot hub. Every time I login "Copilot is ready for you". Every service has pivoted to AI and none of it makes sense! Words have lost meaning! So I'm sticking with "slop" for now :)
This is like declaring that all email is spam because 99% of email is spam. That numbers probably about right (if you turn of spam filters entirely) but it doesn't mean that the remaining 1% is spam.
The same applies to LLM junk these days. I avoid the vast majority of the junk (I'm fortunate to not have to do things like use Microsoft products other than github/vscode, for instance). The majority of what I still see is still junk. That doesn't mean it's all junk though.
To take a public example Daniel Stenberg has been publicly documenting issues with slop bug reports to curl swamping real reports. This is an issue. At the same time in the hands of someone presumably highly competent AI has helped find and fix 70 real bugs, that's not slop, and I'm inclined to believe that Daniel isn't exaggerating here.
This is like declaring that all email is spam because 99% of email is spam.
No it's not. And I think you're making a false equivalence.
I think what @dbushell was trying to convey, using your email analogy, is that they consider that all marketing emails are spam emails. Which I would agree with.
Of course, marketers will try to argue that "cold outreach is the only way to get new customers, the secret to not be disruptive is to target these customers correctly so that they have genuine interest in the product, etc."
But to me, any of these emails is spam. If it's unsolicited, I don't want to receive it, even if my company makes pencils and your company sells refined graphite. If I want to buy refined graphite, I'll google it, and contact you for a quote. I don't want your spam.
The point I'm also trying to make is: everybody is doing some mental gymnastic to try to claim that there is good use of LLMs and bad use of LLMs, using some type of no true scotsman argumentation. If we follow Brandolini's law, in addition of prompting correctly and in a detailed way, you also need check that it didn't output bullshit, a "good use of LLM" should take as much time as you would have spent solving the problem yourself. IMHO, if LLMs were used to speed things up, it's most likely slop, because it wasn't checked as thoroughly as you would have done writing yourself.
I think what @dbushell was trying to convey, using your email analogy, is that they consider that all marketing emails are spam emails. Which I would agree with.
But to me, any of these emails is spam. If it's unsolicited, I don't want to receive it,
I think that analogy works even better than you intended. Both the complaints about spam/marketing and AI slop are about lack of control. You are forced to deal with it. You have to spend time and mental energy without you asking for it, without benefitting you in any way, and benefitting the producer only marginally.
That still doesn't take away that both email and AI can be useful tools. For you. If you have a certain type of problem that can be made smaller by applying AI to it, use it. If you don't experience those problems, then don't. It is a tool, you are responsible for the result. From the outside it should not be obvious that you used the tool. (Talking only about the usefulness here, ethics are something else, of course.)
MHO, if LLMs were used to speed things up, it's most likely slop, because it wasn't checked as thoroughly as you would have done writing yourself.
Not everything needs to be thoroughly checked all the time. Also, this does not consider the situation where one uses generated code as a starting point, simply to get the ball rolling or to familiarize yourself with the problem, and where you would rework the whole codebase several times anyway.
This is like declaring that all email is spam because 99% of email is spam.
No it's not. But if you wanna go that route: Not all code is crappy, just cause 99% of code is crappy.
Not all code is crappy, just cause 99% of code is crappy.
I entirely agree? I don't get your point.
I see your point that "spam" is contextual, but "scam" isn't. An email that will take you to a fake IRS site to steal your identity is always going to be bad because of what it inherently is, regardless of the context. I think "slop" as a term is used the same way a lot of the time. It describes code which is inherently bad, and no amount of contextual appropriateness can de-slop it.
people are still debating whether "software engineering" deserves to be called "engineering"!
I had a bit about that in an earlier draft of my post - about how there are engineers out there from other disciplines who reject software engineering because you don't need a formal license in order to call yourself a software engineer.
I ended up dropping that paragraph in the edit.
you don't need a formal license in order to call yourself a software engineer.
In most countries you don't need a formal license to call yourself a civil engineer either. You need one to be a principal engineer, who can sign off on projects, but the engineers under the principal can call themselves engineers without even needing a diploma.
I'm sure there are purists out there who reject calling any form of software development process "engineering", but I think much of that debate stems from common practices in the software industry, not from specific traits of constructing computer programs.
It's obviously just one data point, so it's only for illustration: I have a formal engineering diploma (which, where I'm from, is a prerequisite for a small number of regulated engineering jobs, but not a prerequisite for most engineering jobs) and I don't think software engineering is a contradiction in terms at all. But I also don't think I've seen anything that can be remotely described as engineering being practised in the vast majority of software teams I've interacted with, in any quality (been part of, consulted for, helped with, knew people from etc.), even though the people who practiced whatever that was, or oversaw it, described it as such.
Freezing software development by requiring "standard engineering practices" would have stifled innovation. Railroads were built and thrived despite steam boilers exploding with alarming regularity. The safety tradeoffs were deemed acceptable.
Maybe in 30 years software practices will have been calcified by GenAI so that you can just specificy "login page" and get it back all tested and done.
I understand what you're saying, and I agree, as in I think that conflating "standard engineering practices" with all of engineering is very unproductive, and that there wouldn't be much to gain from mere standardisation; frankly, I think there wouldn't be that much of a gain even in terms of safety.
But at any given time there are plenty of areas of engineering that are sufficiently new that either no "standard" engineering practices exist, or that some of what you do isn't covered by any standard. I'm sure that's the case with some aspects of railroad engineering today, that's definitely not my specialty. Back when my EE diploma was still fresh enough that I'd know, that was certainly the case with EE. Some of my former colleagues, who got an EE degree because they genuinely wanted to do that, not in order to get better at designing embedded systems, certainly still wrestle with things that aren't "standard" in any way.
IMHO, and paraphrasing a famous definition, the creative application of scientific principles in designing and developing software, the construction and operation of computer software systems with full cognizance of their design, or forecasting their behaviour under specific operation conditions don't specifically require freezing software to any standard engineering practice.
Here is an interesting take on the matter: https://m.youtube.com/watch?v=G7_b3MBSw7w
The TL;DR is, that software mostly is build by craftsmen. A seldom counterexample is building software together with formal proof of correctness, which Boyd claims to be actual engineering.
While I do not agree with some of the statements and the provocative, simplistic communication style (I mistrust most absolute truths), I do see a lot of value in confronting yourself with the question of "What rigor in my work justifies me to feel my self an engineer, not a craftsmen"?
Vibe engineering could be a thing in another 50-100 years, IMO. When I look at our older engineering siblings (most notably Civil Engineering) they have most of their decisions codified into generally accepted engineering practices. They don’t guess, and seldom do calculations ex nihlo, they reduce problems to well-studied base cases and implement solutions based on those parameters. Give software engineering some more time and maturity and I think we could get to a point where an AI is an effective agent of a Professional Engineer applying generally accepted engineering practices. I also think we’re still too Wild West to do that just yet
I get where this is coming from... But I think that it kind of clashes with practices like Extreme Programming and Continuous Discovery. When you get yourself out of the loop of what the agent is produced you are also depriving yourself of those "aha moments" you get when you code yourself. Also I am deeply concerned that AI agents are kind of reinforcing anchor bias. If you don't code yourself you kind of become the architect of your own project. And we know what happens to architecture when it gets separated from software development.. It is a practice you don't see much anymore in software engineering because it has major flaws.
The "aha moments" move up an abstraction layer. I've said it elsewhere, but TL;DR think of the AI Agent not as an independent fire and forget coder, but more of a turbo-charged rubber duck. Ask it questions, make it give you three possible answers, decide on pros and cons, get snippets shown, have it go research for you. Then at some point you'll see the "aha moment" before you wrote the code. Then you can have the LLM make up 80% of it (which is almost always boilerplate -- at least at enterprise level -- that it's quite good at), before it probably gets stuck and you code the final 20%.
If you enjoy the craft of writing code, LLMs will make you sad. If you enjoy the outcomes of winding up the clockwork of a piece of software and actually seeing it go (perhaps at the cost of a bit more time in code review), LLMs are great.
If all LLMs good work is producing boilerplate code I think frameworks are better at this (battle tested and more solid too). There is a certain complexity in software development you can't get rid of. You can hide it under abstractions, but it will haunt you when you don't understand those abstractions. I get that not everybody likes to write code, there are other technical roles that don't require that (there are a lot of devs that are stuck because they don't want to be promoted to e.g. management positions). Instead of managing agents one could manage people, just saying ;)
So this is all nice and well in some idealistic lalaland type situation.
The reality is that LLM technology is more often than not used to squeeze more code output out of mid level engineers and many of these practices (except version control) are aspirational at best.
So the corollary to your article is: Imagine code farms where people are running half a dozen LLMs to produce code using the exact opposite of the practices you just listed.
Simonw and vibecoding becoming my second favorite after andy and oils. Keep those memes coming, bro!
Engineering is the formal practice of problem analysis and design synthesis. While automated tooling for both analysis and synthesis for engineering have existed for decades I'm very troubled by the idea of throwing "prompts" at prediction machines and examining their results as "engineering."
Quantify, qualify, and verify.
Quantify, qualify, and verify sounds like a healthy portion of what I'm trying to get at here. How can we, as professionals, work methodically and produce verified, high quality projects while also taking advantage of the new opportunities these tools provide us?
That's a hard problem! I think it deserves deep levels of thought and exploration.
I agree that it is a hard problem but I don't think that it deserves deep thought and exploration. Even if there was a way to use LLMs to code faster, is there a good reason why we should code faster? And even if there was a good reason why we should code faster, would this reason be compelling enough to overlook the incredible social and ecological harms that LLMs are inflicting to people and the planet?
My personal answer to the first question is "probably not" and to the second one is "definitely not", which is why it always surprises me to see debates about LLMs where people argue about whether or not they can be useful while not even mentioning what for and to what cost.
If we code faster we can build more cool stuff. That's very much a core value of mine - I've spent my whole career seeing ways to ship things faster, from Django onwards.
Why? When has speed to market really made a difference? Because from what I've seen, it doesn't matter that much at all. And I'm looking at Apple, Microsoft, Google, Facebook and Amazon.
I'm an impatient person. There's a lot of stuff I want to build!
Y’know I used to know of you as the person who made Datasette, a thing I thought was cool (though I didn’t ever have a good reason to reach for it). Now I know of you as the guy promoting commercial technology propped up by astonishing amounts of capital which is making many of my peers’ jobs worse and less pleasant.
But how does more cool stuff improve peoples' lives?
I don't want to sound moralising and I respect your values, so I'll just say that the social and ecological utility of my actions has become increasingly important for me recently, and as someone in tech it's sometimes difficult to understand or remember that the world doesn't necessarily need more technology. Trying to find one's place in the world is not easy.
It improves my life, because making things makes me happy.
Part of this that isn’t quite explored is that the time saved on writing code actually enables better engineering practices, in no small part by making tool adoption easier:
I want to say that i like this article and this practice is how I and most of my colleagues use AI. That said, I wouldn't want to miss an argument about terms, so, in a friendly but chiding tone:
"Vibe Coding" sounds unprofessional and silly and has a stigma. I pursuit of legitimizing aspects of the practice you could have chosen either two words to replace. "Coding" - a casual yet recognized term in the software industry, or "Vibe" - modern (and circa woodstock) slang, a casual yet recognized term in the "that guy who just did 3 dabs in a tent at coachella (or woodstock) and now wants your oreos" industry. I'm not sure I would have made that choice 😺
I'm not going to disagree with you on that!
My attempts at getting sensible names to stick have been ab abject failure, so I'm trying something silly instead.
Yeah, keep fighting the good fight on consigning "vibe" to the dumpster of time. Unfortunately it's so catchy I don't think we'll lose it. At least not this generation. Maybe the Zoomers or whoever come after them will think it's lame and drop it themselves.
I hate everything about this. Which probably means it’s the way the industry is going to go.
Why do you hate it?
